html:
Karin Hansdotter, born in Finland in 1539 and died in Närke in 1596, was a landowner in Finland. Her coat of arms featured a pall with three stars, and she is best known for being the official mistress of Duke Johan, later King Johan III, with whom she had four children.

Karin Hansdotter was the daughter of Hans Klasson, a priest in Stockholm, and possibly identical to the monk Hans Kökemäster (Johannes Nicolai Kökemäster, pastor in Stockholm). Her mother was Ingeborg Åkesdotter (Thott), a former nun and illegitimate daughter of Åke Hansson Tott, who lived as a widow from 1564.

== Biography ==

=== Parents and Ancestry === Karin Hansdotter's mother, Ingeborg Åkesdotter, was the daughter of Count Åke Hansson Tott and his mistress, Ingeborg Åkesdotter, who later married a goldsmith and Claes Bardskärare, and was referred to as "well-born." Her father, Hans Klasson Kökkemäster, was likely the son of a burgher in Stockholm of German origin; he was a monk in the Dominican order until the Reformation, when he became a priest. However, he was later removed from his position due to adultery.

Several suggestions have been made regarding who Ingeborg Åkesdotter's father was, but according to reliable sources cited by Hans Gillingstam, her father was named Hans Klasson, and her mother was Ingeborg Åkesdotter, mentioned in a protective letter issued by Erik XIV on June 6, 1564, for the widow and children of Klas Andersson.

In Per Brahe the Elder's correspondence, Ingeborg Åkesdotter is referred to as a "well-born woman," and Greta Wieselgren has suggested that Ingeborg's father was Åke Klasson (Tott), a nephew of Hans Åkesson. Jully Ramsay has speculated that it was Åke Jöransson (Tott), another of Hans Åkesson's nephews.

Anders Sandvig states in his translation, "Historiske Efterretninger om velfortiente Danske Adelsmænd," that Ingeborg Åkesdotter was an illegitimate daughter of Hans Åkesson's notorious son, the Swedish leader Åke Hansson, who died in 1510, "with a woman named Karen," and that Ingeborg's husband, Hans, whose patronymic is not mentioned, was a priest in Stockholm. Sandvig's information is corroborated by both a family tree dating from around 1620 at the Swedish National Archives and genealogical notes recorded by Rasmus Ludvigsson, who was employed by Gustav Vasa and his sons since the 1540s. In these sources, as in the letter from 1564, the father is named Hans Klasson, but his occupation is not specified, while Ingeborg's mother is said to have been named Kerstin Brodersdotter, and her parents are also mentioned. Kerstin's father was reportedly the district judge in Ås district in Västergötland during the late 1400s, Broder Buth, and his wife, Märta Knutsdotter, who belonged to a family that bore a spur over a star on their coat of arms, from which various branches later adopted coats of arms derived through the maternal line, including the names Stenbock and Drake af Intorp.

Hans Gillingstam writes that according to Rasmus Ludvigsson and a contemporary record signed by him, Kerstin Brodersdotter was said to have been married to a Henrik, and that:

Gillingstam believes that the explanation for her coat of arms lies in the following:

Another source from the 17th century mentions Ingeborg Åkesdotter's status as the daughter of Åke Hansson (Tott). This information is found on the last page of folio volume 137 in the Rålambska collection at the Royal Library, and was written by Anna Banér, daughter of Gustav Banér, who was executed in the Linköping Bloodbath in 1600.

Hans Gillingstam concludes his explanation with:

=== Relationships and Children === Karin Hansdotter was employed as a kitchen maid at the court of Katarina Stenbock in 1550, where she met Prince Johan. It is unknown when they began their relationship. According to Lars Olof Larsson, their relationship started sometime in the mid-1550s. In 1555, Johan had established his own household within the castle. She requested to leave her position and, with the permission of her guardian, became Johan's official mistress. In 1556, she was officially recognized as Johan's mistress and lived openly with him in Finland at Turku Castle. In the castle records, she was referred to as "our gracious lady" and had her own court consisting of maids, servants, and friends. Karin followed Johan as far as Arboga when he traveled to England in 1559. She spent the years 1560–1561 with him in Stockholm, and it is said that Karin Hansdotter lived with Duke Johan for at least four years between 1556 and 1561, and they reportedly had four children, of whom the son, Augustus Johansson, died in 1560.

The relationship with Johan ended before his marriage to Katarina Jagellonica. In October 1562, Karin received several estates as a pension, and that same autumn, she married one of Duke Johan's closest friends, his courtier Klas Andersson Westgöthe. She received a dowry consisting of items such as wall hangings, brass candlesticks, and English pewter tableware, and then moved to her husband's estate, Vik in Birkala, together with her mother and her two youngest children, while the oldest remained with Johan.

Duke Johan's conspiracies against his brother, King Erik XIV, led to a rift between the brothers, and in 1563, Karin's husband, Klas Westgöthe, and their two eldest children by Johan were taken captive and brought to Sweden.

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and the perspective of later generations – not least because those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: Karin Hansdotter was a woman who lived in the 16th century and served as the official mistress of Duke John (who later became king). She had four children with him, and upon the end of their relationship, she received substantial gifts and properties as compensation.
lattlast:
Karin Hansdotter was born in Finland during the 16th century. She began working at a court where she met Duke Johan. Because he was a powerful figure, she became his official mistress, which meant she lived with him and had her own staff.

The two lived together for several years and had four children. When Duke Johan was to marry another woman, their relationship ended. Karin then received gifts such as wall hangings and brass candlesticks, as well as several large estates as a pension.

After that, she married one of the duke's closest friends. She then moved to his estate together with her mother and her youngest children. The text also explains that her background is well-documented in old archives, although some details about her family have been difficult for historians to determine precisely.
